goal: Group mainline --help by frequency, hide canonical-hash debug command

summary.title: Group mainline --help by frequency; hide debug command
summary.what: Three cobra Groups added to rootCmd in internal/cli/root.go: Daily (start/append/seal/log/show/status/sync/gaps/check/context/list-proposals), Setup & repair (init/doctor), Advanced (merge/pin/publish/pr-description/thread). canonical-hash command is marked Hidden=true so it stays runnable for debugging but does not appear in --help. Removed the duplicate AddCommand for gapsCmd from gaps.go init() since root.go now owns central registration.
summary.why: mainline --help was a flat alphabetical wall of 19 commands. Best-practice CLI tools (gh, kubectl, docker) use semantic groups so first-time users immediately see what to reach for daily vs what is a fallback. Crucially the split is by FREQUENCY not audience — agents and humans run mostly the same commands per AGENTS.md (status, log, show, start, append, seal, check, context, list-proposals are all agent-used) so an Agent group would have been misleading.
summary.user_goal: New users (humans or agents reading help via cobra --help output) immediately understand which commands matter daily vs which are situational.

decisions.rationale: AGENTS.md instructs agents to run check / context / list-proposals as part of normal work — putting those under an Agent group would imply humans should not run them, which is wrong. The actual question is how often a command is reached for, not by whom.

canonical-hash is a debug utility for inspecting seal hashes; useful to keep available for troubleshooting but not in the user mental model. Hidden flag is the cobra-canonical way to remove from help while preserving runtime behaviour.

The Agent group I tried first was load-bearing only for two commands (context, list-proposals). Per the user reminder, agents also run status/log/show/check/etc. — drawing an Agent boundary is artificial. Three groups is enough resolution and matches how AGENTS.md describes the surface.

rejected.reason: Agents and humans run mostly the same commands; the boundary is artificial and would mislead readers about which commands they should use.
Still useful for debugging seal hashes when investigating audit-trail issues; hide-from-help is the right level.
cobra auto-adds it and the convention across cobra-based tools is to leave it visible. Diverging would break shell-completion discoverability.

risks: Hidden=true on canonical-hash means new contributors discovering the codebase via --help would not see it; documented in the code comment so it remains discoverable via grep / source reading.

Group titles use slightly long labels (e.g. "Advanced (manual fallbacks; auto-flow normally handles these):"). Cobra renders them verbatim; on narrow terminals they may wrap. Acceptable: signal value of the parenthetical caveat outweighs the cosmetic risk.
followups: When new commands are added, set GroupID on creation so they slot into one of the three groups (or explicitly Hidden=true). Without this, new commands fall into "Additional Commands" — a Cobra default category that includes help / completion.
Consider whether help and completion (currently Additional Commands) should be folded into Setup or hidden entirely. Pre-existing cobra defaults; not part of this change.

architectural_claims: CLI help output is grouped by frequency (Daily / Setup / Advanced) at the root command level via cobra.Group.
Debug-only commands use Hidden=true to remain runnable while not appearing in help.
behavioral_changes: `mainline --help` now shows 3 grouped sections + 1 Additional Commands cobra default + Hidden canonical-hash, instead of one alphabetical list of 19.
`mainline canonical-hash` continues to work but is no longer surfaced in help.

api_changes.signature: mainline --help output reorganised into 3 groups (Daily / Setup / Advanced)
mainline canonical-hash hidden from help (still runnable)
